deck_title: Why Japan and South Korea Want Both U.S. Security Insurance and a China Interface
page_plan_goal: Translate the classroom-friendly outline into page-level implementation decisions with clarity-first layouts and minimal visual complexity.

density_review.reason: The classroom-friendly outline is already paced for a 15-minute presentation. No slide requires splitting if timeline, comparison, and synthesis pages are rendered as structured visuals rather than dense bullet lists.

slides.title: Why Do Japan and South Korea Want Both?

major_blocks.content: Main title in large type
Short framing line: U.S. security insurance and China interface
One-sentence hook stating that the issue is alliance management, not simple side-choosing

slides.speaker_goal: Hook the room immediately and define the talk as a strategic puzzle rather than a descriptive chronology.

density_check.reason: Cover slide remains sparse by design.

slides.title: One Region, Two Tracks

major_blocks.content: Timeline title and short orientation sentence
U.S.-Japan-ROK security lane with 2023-2025 milestones
China-facing/CJK lane with 2024-2025 milestones
Simple color code distinguishing reassurance track and interface track
One-line conclusion: the region moved into a dual-track structure

slides.speaker_goal: Give the audience one clean empirical map before theory and cases begin.

density_check.reason: Potentially dense, but manageable if rendered as a two-lane SVG with short labels instead of bullets.

slides.title: Key Idea: Alliance Management, Not Simple Side-Choosing

major_blocks.content: Theory title
Simple visual showing abandonment on one side and entrapment on the other
Why stronger U.S. reassurance reduces abandonment
Why a China interface reduces entrapment and instability

slides.speaker_goal: Give the audience the minimum theory needed to understand every slide that follows.

density_check.reason: Conceptual slide stays readable if the theory is reduced to one diagram and two short applications.

slides.title: Japan Case 1: Why Tokyo Wanted Harder U.S. Insurance

major_blocks.content: Main claim: Japan sought more institutional and predictable U.S. commitments
Camp David
U.S.-Japan extended deterrence ministerial statement
Guidelines for Extended Deterrence
Small label tying the slide to abandonment reduction

slides.speaker_goal: Show that Japan was trying to make U.S. reassurance more visible, formal, and durable.

density_check.reason: Three evidence cards are enough; adding more would overload the slide.

slides.title: Japan Case 2: Why Tokyo Still Kept China Channels Open

major_blocks.content: Japan-China defense hotline as crisis-management interface
High-level economic dialogue as controlled economic-governance interface
Bottom strip noting that the interface is narrow, not trust-based

slides.speaker_goal: Prove that the Japan case is not pure containment and that interface preservation is part of the strategy.

density_check.reason: Only two evidence blocks plus one interpretive note; no split needed.

slides.title: South Korea Case 1: Why Seoul Wanted Even More Reassurance

major_blocks.content: Main claim: South Korea pushed for even more visible reassurance than Japan
Washington Declaration
NCG institutionalization
Strategic-asset visibility and planning
Interpretive tag: stronger nuclear anxiety produces stronger reassurance demands

slides.speaker_goal: Make the audience see why the Korean case is a stronger reassurance case, not just another copy of Japan.

density_check.reason: Vertical sequencing makes the evidence feel cumulative without requiring a separate slide.

slides.title: South Korea Case 2: Why Seoul Also Kept a China Interface

major_blocks.content: Why China still matters: North Korea, regional stability, economic exposure
Korea-China foreign ministers' meeting as evidence of preserved interface
One-line inference: Seoul wanted stronger insurance without erasing diplomatic room

slides.speaker_goal: Show that South Korea's China interface is structurally thicker than Japan's, not just tactically convenient.

density_check.reason: The left side carries causes and the right side carries evidence; the balance is manageable.

slides.title: Japan and South Korea: Same Dilemma, Different Balance

major_blocks.content: Japan
South Korea
Security insurance logic
China interface logic
Main strategic driver
Bottom-line characterization: balancing-heavy vs interface-heavy

slides.speaker_goal: Give the audience one memorable side-by-side slide that locks in the two-case distinction.

density_check.reason: A four-row matrix is readable at 16:9; adding more categories would require a split.

slides.title: What This Says About Northeast Asia

major_blocks.content: Regional-order title
Two-track order diagram showing hard-security lane and China-facing governance/interface lane
Japan and South Korea positioned between the two tracks
One-line order claim: bifurcated by function, not fully split by membership

slides.speaker_goal: Scale the two country cases up into one clear regional-order interpretation.

density_check.reason: This would be too dense as bullets, but one explanatory SVG keeps it to a single slide.

slides.title: Did the Pattern Stop in 2025?

major_blocks.content: U.S.-Japan-ROK track after 2025: continued deepening
CJK track after 2025: slower but still functioning
Validation sentence explaining why the main 2023-2025 argument still stands

slides.speaker_goal: Reassure the audience that the argument is not frozen in 2025 and that later evidence supports the pattern.

density_check.reason: A two-card validation format is enough because this is a checking slide, not a new analytical section.

slides.title: Final Takeaway

major_blocks.content: Japan and South Korea use two institutional tracks to manage two strategic risks
Track one: U.S. security insurance
Track two: China-facing interface

slides.speaker_goal: Leave the class with the shortest defensible thesis they can remember after the presentation ends.

density_check.reason: Conclusion slide should stay sparse and memorable.
